mainText:
Good morning. Here's your Breeze communications briefing for Tuesday, March 31.

Breeze made news today on Long Island. Greater Long Island reported that the airline is adding a new BreezeThru connection from Long Island MacArthur Airport, with flights routing through Myrtle Beach before continuing to Akron-Canton Airport in Ohio. Service begins May sixth, with fares starting at one hundred seventy-five dollars each way and flights operating twice weekly. The route expands Breeze's secondary-market flythrough strategy and gives communications a fresh angle heading into spring booking season — particularly for reaching the New York metro area, where Breeze has been quietly building a strong MacArthur hub.

Now for industry and competitor news.

C.N.N. reported today that T.S.A. officers are now six weeks into the partial D.H.S. shutdown and have missed three paychecks, with more than five hundred resignations, record callout rates approaching twelve percent, and the F.A.A. now capping daily national airspace operations by two hundred eighty flights. D.H.S. Secretary Kristi Noem was fired by President Trump as the crisis intensified, and while workers received partial back pay Monday, union officials say security lines could stay disrupted for weeks after any resolution. This is Breeze's most immediate operational context — the question is whether the secondary airports in the Breeze network are experiencing lighter disruption than major hubs, and whether a proactive communications posture on the passenger experience at Breeze gates is worth pursuing this week.

Euronews reported Monday and Airlive reported today that jet fuel prices have more than doubled in roughly one month, climbing to nearly two hundred dollars per barrel due to the Iran conflict, with United Airlines having already cut five percent of its capacity and aviation analysts warning a critical shortage could hit major hubs within days. For Breeze, this is a potential competitive opening — as larger carriers trim off-peak flying, some secondary-market point-to-point routes may see reduced competition, and now is an ideal moment to emphasize the A two-twenty fleet's fuel-efficiency credentials to both travelers and the media.

Airlive reported Monday that Sixty Minutes aired previously unreleased footage of the January twenty-twenty-five midair collision between an American Airlines regional jet and a Black Hawk helicopter that killed sixty-seven people near Reagan National Airport, while the segment revealed the F.A.A. had already issued a new rule in mid-March permanently banning visual separation between fixed-wing aircraft and helicopters at all high-traffic airports nationwide. Leadership should confirm operational awareness of the new F.A.A. rule and assess whether any Breeze airports with helicopter traffic near approach corridors warrant a review or passenger communication.

I.A.T.A. released February demand data today showing global air passenger demand grew six point one percent year over year, with load factors reaching a record eighty-one point four percent, though Director General Willie Walsh warned that rising fuel costs and the Middle East war are already slowing capacity planning on affected routes. North America posted two point eight percent growth — a healthy baseline heading into spring — which supports Breeze's secondary-market thesis and suggests underlying demand remains robust even as operational headwinds mount.

Airlive reported today that Delta flight D.L. one-forty-seven, an Airbus A three-fifty operating from Atlanta to Santiago, declared a general emergency and diverted safely to Antofagasta, Chile — the second Delta incident in South America in two days, following an uncontained engine failure the day before. The cause remains unknown, but elevated media scrutiny on airline safety incidents could increase passenger questions industrywide, and it may be worth briefing frontline communications staff.

That's your briefing. Roger is standing by when you're ready to dive deeper or take action on any of these stories.
